coredata.dc:description: The M−CQDs/ZnO nanorods (NRs) heterostructure thin films- based UV Photodetector devices exhibit the photoresponse at zero-bias (0 V) under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm (1, 3 and 5 mW/cm2). The band-edge PL emission (381 nm) intensity of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films is decreasing as compared with ZnO seed layer film, ascribed to the reduced charge recombination which results in the increased photocurrent. The XPS studies ascertain the presence of chemically adsorbed M−CQDs (chemisorption) on ZnO NRs by shifting of peaks to the higher binding energy side. The UV photodetector using M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure exhibits the higher photocurrent density of 123.49 μA/cm2 with photoresponsivity of 81.2 mA/W and external quantum efficiency of 27.6 % at the bias of 1 V under 1 mW/cm2, as compared with ZnO NRs (93.5 μA/cm2) and seed layer ZnO devices (1.17 μA/cm2). It also exhibited the improved photoresponse (-0.5 nA) at 0 V, which is 1.5 times higher than that (-0.33 nA) of ZnO NRs based device under 1 mW/cm2, showing its application in self-powered devices.

(ii) UV–visible transmittance spectra of the pristine seed layer ZnO film (a), ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) with inset of Tauc plot showing the optical bandgap of the films (a − e).
(i) The XRD pattern of M−CQDs powder. (ii) The XRD spectra and (iii) Texture coefficients of pristine seed layer ZnO film (a), ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e).
(i) HR-TEM image with inset showing the d-spacing, (ii) Particle size distribution, and (iii) EDAX spectrum of M−CQDs powder.
FESEM images and Elemental compositions (EDS) of hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs film (b) (i) and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [c (ii) and e (iii)].
The EDS elemental mapping of constituent elements (Zn, O, C) in the hydrothermally-grown ZnO NRs film (b) and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c and e).
FTIR [(i) – (iii)] and Raman [(iv) – (vi)] spectra of M−CQDs powder, the pristine seed layer ZnO film (a), ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e).
The XPS (wide) spectrum (i) and narrow scan spectra of (ii) C 1s and (iii) O 1s of M−CQDs powder.

The XPS wide scan spectra (i) and the narrow scan core level spectra [(ii) Zn 2p (iii) C 1s and (iv) O 1s] of the pristine seed layer ZnO film (a), ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c and e).

The PL emission (i) and Normalized PL (ii) spectra of M−CQDs solution with various excitation wavelengths from 320 to 420 nm. The PL emission spectra [(iii) λex = 325 nm] and normalized PL emission spectra [(iv) λex = 365 nm] of pristine seed layer ZnO film (a), ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e).
The Device structure and energy level diagram of (i) ZnO NRs [film (b) in device B and (ii) M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [(c − e) in devices (C − E)].

The J − V characteristics (Current density − Voltage) of the pristine seed layer ZnO [film (a) in device A], ZnO NRs [(film (b) in device B)], and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [(c − e) in devices (C − E)] under dark condition (i) and the UV illumination (365 nm) of 1 mW/cm2 (ii).

The photoreponse (I − t) characteristics of pristine seed layer ZnO [film (a) in device A], ZnO NRs [film (b) in device B], and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [(c − e) in devices (C − E)] upon switching of UV illumination (365 nm) of 1 mW/cm2 for every 30 secs at 1 V.

(i). Responsivity (Rλ), (ii). External quantum efficiency (EQE), and (iii). Detectivity (Dλ) of the pristine seed layer ZnO [film (a) in device A], ZnO NRs [film (b) in device B], M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [(c − e) in devices (C − E)] respectively under UV illumination (365 nm) of 1 mW/cm2 .

The photoreponse (I − t) characteristics of the pristine seed layer ZnO [film (a) in device A], ZnO NRs [film (b) in device B], M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films [(c − e) in devices (C − E)] at 0 V (self-power mode) under the UV illumination of 365 nm with different light intensities of 1, 3 and 5 mW/cm2, respectively. The schematic representation of (i) the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs and (ii) increased interface potential barrier at the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterojunction in M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films.
The parameters of UV photodetector based on Organic/Inorganic heterostructures (ZnO and CQDs) (published elsewhere).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots derived from mango-leaves (M−CQDs): M−CQDs/ZnO nanorods heterostructure thin films for efficient self-powered UV photodetector applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure based Self-Powered UV Photodetector photoresponse.

The M−CQDs/ZnO nanorods (NRs) heterostructure thin films- based UV Photodetector devices exhibit the photoresponse at zero-bias (0 V) under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm (1, 3 and 5 mW/cm2). The band-edge PL emission (381 nm) intensity of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films is decreasing as compared with ZnO seed layer film, ascribed to the reduced charge recombination which results in the increased photocurrent. The XPS studies ascertain the presence of chemically adsorbed M−CQDs (chemisorption) on ZnO NRs by shifting of peaks to the higher binding energy side. The UV photodetector using M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure exhibits the higher photocurrent density of 123.49 μA/cm2 with photoresponsivity of 81.2 mA/W and external quantum efficiency of 27.6 % at the bias of 1 V under 1 mW/cm2, as compared with ZnO NRs (93.5 μA/cm2) and seed layer ZnO devices (1.17 μA/cm2). It also exhibited the improved photoresponse (-0.5 nA) at 0 V, which is 1.5 times higher than that (-0.33 nA) of ZnO NRs based device under 1 mW/cm2, showing its application in self-powered devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The population and global energy consumption have grown drastically in recent decades which have prompted advancements in sustainable and energy-efficient optical energy detecting technologies. Among those energy detection systems, currently Ultra violet (UV) photodetector (PD) are very much needed for the UV detection. Numerous scientific and industrial applications such as optical communications, flame detection and bio-imaging are depended on the UV photodetectors [1–5]. There are two types of UV PDs: conventional and self-powered. (i) Conventional devices need external bias voltage for the separation of photogenerated charge carriers (electrons and holes). This process consumes energy and increases the size of the device which limits both the downsizing and impacting large scale arrays [1,6]. (ii) Self-powered photodetector works without an applied external electric field, meeting the requirements of small size, less weight, and low power consumption. These are widely examined for next generation nanoscale devices [4,7]. Nowadays, most of the detectors are based on the low bandgap (indirect) Si and communal photomultipliers (PMT’s) which are expensive and thus it is need of the hour to be replaced with low-cost detectors using wide bandgap semiconductor materials like ZnO, TiO2, GaN, SnO2 [1,2]. The Zinc oxide (ZnO) has emerged as a leading material for numerous optoelectronic devices and UV photodetectors because of good characteristics including its wide band gap (3.3 eV), high mobility, high exciton binding energy (60 meV), non-toxicity, earth abundant, highly transparent, and solution processed. ZnO nanostructures are known to exhibit good UV photodetector performance because of their huge surface to volume ratio and ability to efficiently increase the light absorption capacity which significantly improve the photocurrent by adsorption and desorption process [8,9]. The spin coated high quality ZnO thin film-based metal–semiconductor-metal (MSM) UV photodetector exhibited responsivity of 4.3 A/W, good stability, and sensitivity of 43 % at 5 V under 365 nm UV illumination with 0.66 μW/cm2 light intensity [10]. Li et al. has prepared the ZnO/Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate)(PEDOT:PSS) heterojunction structure and ZnO nanorods arrays (annealed under hydrogen ambient at 300 °C) based self-powered UV photodetector exhibited the highest photocurrent of 80 nA and high photo-sensitivity (1000) at zero bias under 365 nm with 2 mW/cm2 light intensity [11]. Chai et al. has reported that the ZnO nanorod based UV photodetector showed the responsivity of 15 mA/W at bias of 0.1 V under UV light (361 nm) [12]. The hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) dissolved DI water-grown ZnO NRs based UV photodetector showed the high sensitivity (1.67 × 104) with the rise/decay time of 32/130 s [13]. Abbasi et al. has also prepared the ZnO thin films using thermal decomposition process and reported that their UV photodetectors showed the higher responsivity (3.42 μA/W) with rise/decay time of 18/25 s at a bias of 5 V [14]. The laterally-grown ZnO nanowire-based UV photodetectors revealed the responsivity of 6.04 mA/W under incident UV light of 350 nm at 0.1 V [15]. The direct vapour phase prepared ZnO nanostructures consisting of microspheres in a network of nanowires exhibited the photoresponsivity of 50 mA/W at 1 V under the UV illumination of 385 nm [16]. Jun et al. has also demonstrated the high photocurrent to dark current ratio of 106 and responsivity of 0.1 mA/W with rise/decay time of 48/0.9 s using the UV photodetectors based on ZnO nanoparticles under the UV exposure (380 nm) of 3 mW/cm2 at 1 V [17].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) play a major role in improving the UV photodetector’s performance due to increased active surface sites, showing better advantages compared to the conventional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots like CdSe, PbS QDs etc [18]. Natural precursors are beneficial because they can convert biomass waste into useful and valuable products and are renewable and biocompatible. Nowadays most of the researchers have been attracted to prepare the natural-derived carbon quantum dots due to the clean process of easily acquirable, earth abundant and environmental friendly. Some of such examples are pomelo fruit [19], oranges, watermelon[20], gram peel, plant leaves, sugarcane [21], kelp and coriander leaves used in different optoelectronic and sensor applications [22,23]. Sahu et al. has reported the synthesis of orange juice-derived CQDs for bio-imaging applications [24]. Their particle size, structure and optical properties mostly depend on the precursor material and synthesis procedure. CQDs can act as the electron donor and electron acceptor material to comprehend the fluorescent detection purposes which exhibit the absorption of light as well as the quick collection of excitons (electron − hole pairs) engendered through photoelectricity. The CQDs are essential to produce improved photocurrents because they promote carrier photomultification which gives the significant amount of broad-banding photosensing [25,26]. The solution processed N-CQDs incorporated ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin film-based UV photodetector (365 nm, 1 mW/cm2) demonstrated the higher responsivity of 9 mA/W, detectivity of 5 × 1011 Jones, and the rise and decay times (13.2 and 17 s) at 1 V with 1 mW/cm2 (365 nm). It also exhibited the improved performances due to the efficient transfer of photogenerated charge carriers from N-CQDs to ZnO [27]. The CQDs-ZnO nanoarrays (ZnO NRs)/Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenophene) (PEDOS)/FTO based organic/inorganic face-to-face UV photodetector performance observed to be excellent under 0.32 mW cm−2 (365 nm) at zero voltage (0 V), showing 22.62 mA/W and rise time/decay time of 0.09 s/0.12 s [28]. The hydrothermally grown ZnO nanorods (ZNRs) arrays decorated with N-CQDs (N-CQDs/ZNRs) hybrid thin film-based UV photodetector (365 nm, 3 mW/cm2) revealed 142.4 mA/W and 48.4 % at 8 V [29]. The orange juice derived carbon nanodots (CNDs)/n-si heterojunction UV photodetectors showed the high photoresponsivity of 1.23 mA/W at −4 V under 300 nm UV illumination and it exhibited the lower dark current and good rectification ratio (∼5 × 103) [30]. The PEDOT:PSS/CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid structure-based UV photodetector exhibited a detectivity of 8.33 × 1012 Jones which was higher than that (6.18 × 1012 Jones) of the PEDOT:PSS/PVK/ZnO NRs hybrid UV photodetector [31]. It has also been reported that the CQDs enable electron-hole separation in the device under UV illumination with reduced leakage current and efficient charge transfer to ZnO NRs. The comparison of the device structure and their performances of various UV photodetectors reported elsewhere are given in the Table 1 .

In this work we prepared hydrothermally grown ZnO nanorods (NRs) and natural derived carbon quantum dots from mango leaves (M−CQDs) using the cost-effective pyrolysis method. The hydrothermally prepared ZnO nanorods (NRs) films were immersed into M−CQDs solution at various time periods of 1, 2, and 3 h to get the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films. We studied the morphological, optical, structural, and electrical properties of the prepared heterostructure films. UV photodetectors were fabricated by depositing Al metal contacts onto the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films and the device performances were characterized. M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure based device exhibits the improved photoresponse at 0 V bias useful for commercial self-powered UV PD applications.

The preparation of CQDs from mango leaves is as-such followed as reported elsewhere [32] and the work flow is given in the order as (1) Mango leaves were washed with DI water and then shredded; (2) then the shredded leaves were heated for 3 h at 280 °C in an oven to completely evaporate the moisture; (3) the dried leaves were crushed into powder in mortar; (4) After that the obtained leaves powder (3 g) and H2O2 (10 mL) were added in 200 mL DI water and stirred at room temperature for 12 h; (5) the solution was filtered with filter paper using vacuum filtration to remove precipitation and purified [32]. The acquired CQDs exhibited bright blue under the UV illumination.

Zinc acetate dehydrate [Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O; 99.5 %; Sigma Aldrich] was used as a Zinc precursor. 2-methoxyethanol [C3H8O2; 99.5 %; Sigma Aldrich] as the solvent and mono ethanolamine (MEA) [C2H7NO; 99.5 %; Finar] as the stabilizer were used. The preparation of ZnO seed layer solution was reported in our previous works published elsewhere [27,33,34]. The ZnO precursor mixture was stirred at 65 ℃ for 2 h to yield a homogeneous solution and allowed to aging for 24 h. Then, the ZnO seed layer was deposited by using the spin coating on the glass substrates with a spin speed of 2000 rpm for 30 s and was post annealed for 10 min at 180 ℃ and the same process is repeated for 5 times to get the optimal thickness of 200 nm. The deposited ZnO seed layers were a post annealed at 350 °C for 2 h in tubular furnace to improve the film uniformity and crystallinity and the deposited pristine ZnO seed layer is denoted as film (a) [33,34].

ZnO NRs were grown by using the hydrothermal process. 0.05 M Zinc nitrate hexahydrate [Zn (NO3)2·6H2O.; 99.5 %; SRL] and 0.05 M of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) [C6H12N4; 99.5 %; Sigma Aldrich] were dissolved in 100 mL of DI water and stirred for 1 h at normal ambient. After that the solution transferred into Teflon lined auto-clave and the seeded glass substrates were dipped into the solution, then the auto-clave was placed in the oven at 95 °C for 4 h. The grown ZnO NRs were cleaned with DI water, ethanol and followed by the annealing under the normal ambient for 2 h at 400 °C to enhance the crystalline nature of ZnO NRs [film (b)] [29,35,36].

The hydrothermally grown ZnO nanorod films were immersed into M−CQDs solution for 1 h (film c), 2 h (film d) and 3 h (film e). After completion of the immersing process, the obtained M−CQDs/ZnO heterostructure thin films (c − e) were annealed for 1 h at 110 °C to evaporate the solvent residue on the films. The reaction mechanism of growth of ZnO nanorods is given below [33,35,36].

The transmittance spectra of the ZnO (film a), hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs film (b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) were obtained using Agilent Carry 5000 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer. The XRD (PAN analytical Netherlands, XPERT-PRO model) data were collected using the X-ray source (Cu K-α: 1.5406 Å) at 45 kV and 30 mA. The X’Pert Highscore Plus software was used to refine the XRD data. The HR-TEM images of prepared quantum dots powder using pyrolysis method were captured by using ThermoFisher Scientific, Talos F200 S model. The FE-SEM (JEOL JSM IT- 800) coupled with energy dispersive X-Ray spectrometer (EDS) was used to study the surface morphology and elemental mapping analysis. The FT-IR spectroscopy (JASCO Co. Japan, FT/IR-6600 model) was used to study the films (a − e). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 versa probe III) using a monochromatized X-ray source (Al Kα radiation; hν = 1486.6 eV) was used to analyse the elemental compositions after doing the charge correction with reference to C 1s peak (284 eV) of pristine ZnO thin film (a) for all core level spectra of hybrid films (b, c, and e). Raman spectra of the pristine ZnO (film a), ZnO NRs and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (b − e) were acquired from the Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France). The spectrometer Horiba Jobin-Yvon, Fluorolog3 was used to study the emission characteristics of the films (a−e).

Aluminium (Al) metal contacts were deposited onto the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films by using the thermal evaporation method (VR technologies, Bangalore, India) with metal mask at high vacuum of 5.5 × 10−5 mbar (thickness 120 nm) and the active area of the device is 0.08 cm2 (0.4 cm × 0.2 cm). The current versus voltage (I − V) and time dependent photoresponse (photocurrent versus time) (I − t) measurements of the fabricated UV photodetectors using the film a (Device A), ZnO NRs thin film b (Device B), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films [c (Device C), d (Device D), and e (Device E)] were carried out using the Keithley source meter (2450 model) under the 365 nm UV spot light (Hamamastu, Japan, L9588- 06A Model) at 1, 3, and 5 mW/cm2.

The absorption characteristics of natural carbon quantum dots derived from mango leaves (M−CQDs) by using pyrolysis method and the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (a − e) are shown in the Fig. 1 . The optical absorption spectrum of M−CQDs solution showed the broad absorption wavelength at 200–700 nm in the Fig. 1(i). It exhibits the broad peak wavelength at 213 nm which is assigned to the π-π* transition due to the excitation of sp2 electrons in the graphitic C–C carbon domain state [32,37]. The optical bandgap of M−CQDs is determined to be 3.57 eV using the tauc plot. Mostly the bandgap is exaggerated through the segment of sp2 domains, their shape, and size. As-prepared carbon quantum dots are brown in colour in (day light) and when illuminating with UV light, the solution colour changed into blue emission, ascribed to the quantum confinement effect, surface functional groups, and the optical absorption of CQDs inter-connected to π-plasmon transitions in their core structure [33]. The photogenerated electrons and holes were trapped at different surface locations and undergone the radiative recombination responsible for the fluorescence emissions in the visible to near-IR [38]. The transmittance spectra of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (a − e) are shown in the Fig. 1(ii). The ZnO thin film (a) exhibited the higher characteristic transmittance of 89.4 % at 550 nm (average transmittance of 84.9 %). It is found that of ZnO NRs film (b) is increased to 90.4 % (84 % of average transmittance) and that of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs (c − e) heterostructure films is slightly decreased [90 % (82 %; average transmittance) for film (c), 89 % (85 %) for film (d) and 87.6 % (78.9 %) for film (e) at 550 nm], ascertaining that the adsorbed CQDs onto the surface of ZnO NRs and/or grain boundaries are acting as light absorbing and scattering centres [29,33,34]. The optical band gap (Eg) of the sol–gel derived spin-coated as-prepared ZnO (film a), ZnO NRs (film b), and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) is determined by using following Tauc relation.
α h ν
2

A
( E - h ν )
Where α is the optical absorption coefficient, hν is the energy of incident photon and A is constant. Inset of the Fig. 1(ii) shows tauc plots of the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films [3.29 (a), 3.28 (b), 3.282 (c), 3.291 (d) and 3.27 eV (e)]. The slight changes in the optical bandgap of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films indicate the existence of CQDs onto ZnO NRs, ascertaining the quantum confinement effect [27,29].

The synthesized M−CQDs and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (a − e) has revealed XRD pattern as shown in the Fig. 2 . M−CQDs powder exhibits broad peak at 22˚ due to the amorphous nature (containing more oxygen molecules) and the surface defects of carbon quantum dots as shown in the Fig. 2(i) [21]. The growth orientation, crytal structure and phase identification of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (a − e) exhibit the hexagonal wurtizite structure with c − axis orientation (002) peak (Fig. 2(ii)). The observed diffraction peaks at the 2θ angle of 31.77, 34.31, 36.29, 47.42, 56.62, 62.56 and 67.88˚ are corresponding to the hkl planes of (100), (002), (101), (102), (110) and (103), respectively. After decoration of M−CQDs onto ZnO suraface, there is no additional peaks related to M−CQDs due to less crystallinity of M−CQDs and no impurity peaks are also observed [39]. The slight shifitng of (002) and (101) planes of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films upon increasing of M−CQDs content (from film c to e) to lower angle side may be ascribed to the adsorption of M−CQDs onto the boundaries of ZnO grains [33,40]. Moreover, it confirms that CQDs and ZnO nanorods are having strong physical interaction along the plane having higher surface energy [29]. There is no additional peaks but increased intensity of (002) peak in the film (e) when compared to that of film c and d which may be due to the adsorption of excess amount of M−CQDs on the surface of ZnO NRs [29]. The oberved slight peak shifts to the lower angle side in film (d) may be ascribed to the strain or strees induced at the surface of ZnO NRs and/or chemical interactions between ZnO and M−CQDs. The preferred growth orientation of films was estimated using the texture coefficient.

Where Ihkl is the measured intensity of the assigned hkl plane, Iohkl denotes the standard peak intensity of the hkl plane from the JCPDS file, and N is the total number of diffraction peaks detected in the corresponding film's XRD pattern. As shown in Fig. 2(iii), the film (a) is highly textured and orientated along the (002) diffraction plane. This may be attributed to the fact that the c − axis (002) growth orientation is facilitated by the increased strain created between the ZnO film and substrate. The grown ZnO NRs in the film (b) and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) exhibit the higher texture orientation along the c − axis peak (002) as compared with pristine film (a). The increased TC value of (002) plane reveals that the unique crystalline arrangement and due to the surface properties of nanorods [33,34]. M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) revealed that the TC intensity of (002) plane is increased and reduced for (100) and (101) planes. This may be attributed to the M−CQDs got adsorbed onto the ZnO NRs surface. The enhancement in the (002) plane texture coefficient in film c reveals a good crystalline arrangement due to the surface states in the nanorods [33,34].

The morphology and particulate size of natural derived carbon quantum dots from mango leaves (M−CQDs) were analysed by using the High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Fig. 3 (i) showed the aggregates irregular spherical shape of M−CQDs, attributed to the amorphous nature of carbon (more oxygen contained molecules) [41]. Fig. 3(ii) shows the histogram of the average size (2–3 nm) of M−CQDs. The inset of Fig. 3(i) showed the lattice fringes with interplanar distance of about 0.254 nm, indicating the graphitic core positioning of the M−CQDs [41]. The elements of carbon and oxygen were confirmed by EDAX spectra as shown in the Fig. 3(iii) [32,41]. FE-SEM images (top view) at the magnification of 100 nm and 50 nm reveal the surface morphology of hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs film (b) and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (c and e) as given in the Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(i) confirmed the presence of vertically grown hexagonal ZnO nanorods (NRs) (film (b)) with average diameter of 24 nm (Inset is showing the histogram of nanorods by using the ImageJ software). Fig. 4(ii) reveals the morphological features of film (c) with the presence of small white patches onto the ZnO NRs surface, resulting in a slight increase in the average diameter (29 nm) of NRs than that of film (b) and ascertaining the adsorption of M−CQDs onto ZnO nanorod surface [29,33]. The film (e) shows the randomly oriented ZnO NRs which may be ascribed to the aggregation of CQDs onto the ZnO nanorod surface, resulting in decreased average diameter of around 20 nm as compared with films b and c (shown as inset of Fig. 4(iii)) [29,33]. The constituent elements such as Zn, O and C of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterojunction have been ascertained from the EDS studies. The film (b) exhibits the elemental weight percentage of 83.03 % (Zn) and 16.97 % (O), whereas the film (c) showed the slightly decreased percentage of 82.37 % (Zn) and 16.78 % (O) along with the presence of 0.86 % (C). The film (e) reveals the increased carbon content (1.68 %) with a decreased Zn (82.65 %), O (15.67 %) concentration upon increasing the M−CQDs amount (for increased immersion time scale), ascertaining the adsorption of M−CQDs onto the ZnO NRs surface [29]. The EDS elemental mapping analysis shows the uniform distribution of constituent elements (Zn and O) in ZnO film and (Zn, O and C) in M−CQDs/ZnO heterostructure thin films (Fig. 5 ), supporting the presence of M−CQDs onto the surface of ZnO NRs.

FT-IR spectrum reveals the surface structural changes and the functional groups of M−CQDs powder and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (a − e) as shown in the Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(i) shows the bands [3331 cm−1 assigned to O–H, 2925 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 to asymmetric peaks of C–H, 1608 cm−1 to C = O, 1384 cm−1 to C = C, and 1090 cm−1 to C-O] corresponding to distinctive functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl & carboxyl) and related stretching vibrations present in the prepared M−CQDs [19,33]. Fig. 6(ii) shows the broad strong absorption peak (400–600 cm−1) centred at 405 cm−1 related to Zn-O band due to the stretching vibrations in the pristine seed layer (film a). The absorption band at 923 cm−1 [C–H bending vibrational mode] along with the weak bands at around 1595/2191 (C = C stretching), 1740 cm−1 to the carboxyl group (C = O stretching), 2856 and 2924 cm−1 (C–H stretching), and 3421 cm−1 to hydroxyl group (O–H stretching) may be attributed to the residue formed from the precursors solution and while coating under the normal ambient [33]. Film (b) exhibits the broad absorption band (400–600 cm−1) centred at 404 cm−1, ascribing to the stretching vibrations of Zn-O in the hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs. The intensity of absorption band at 884 cm−1 (attributed to the C–H bending vibrational mode) is observed to be increased as compared with film a due to high surface area of ZnO NRs. The absorption bands of the functional groups corresponding to 1595/2190 (C = C stretching), 1795 [carboxyl group (C = O)], 2856/2928 (C–H stretching), and 3426 cm−1 (O–H stretching) are found to be slightly shifted to higher frequency side, ascribed to the transfer of oxygen atoms to ZnO NRs during the hydrothermal process and/or the improved adhesion of ZnO seed layer during spin coating process [42]. The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) showed all IR absorption bands of the functional groups corresponding to ZnO observed in the film b but slightly shifted to the lower frequency side after immersion into the M−CQDs. This shifting may be attributed to the surface states of M−CQDs and chemical interaction/covalent bonding between ZnO NRs and M−CQDs [19]. The Fig. 6(iii) reveals the slightly enhanced absorption intensity of the C–H vibration band at 886 cm−1 upon increasing the immersion time as compared with film b, attributed to the increasing content of M−CQDs onto the surface of ZnO NRs in the films (c − e). This may be ascribed to the structural changes, induced strain/stress effect, and the chemical interaction between M−CQDs and ZnO nanorod surface [19,33]. These results indicate that the adsorption (via chemical interaction-chemisorption) of M−CQDs onto the ZnO NRs surface, facilitating the efficient energy transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO [33,43].

Raman spectroscopy is an effective tool to analyse various parameters of thin films in particular such as crystal structure, quality, chemical compositions, and purity of sample. Fig. 6 also reveals the Raman spectra of M−CQDs powder and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (a − e) using excitation wavelength at 633 nm (He-Ne laser). Fig. 6(iv) exhibits the two peaks at 1356 cm−1 (attributed to D band due to the sp3 hybridization of C atoms) and 1576 cm−1 of the G band (due to vibrations of sp2 hybridized C–C bonds acquired from the core hexagonal structure) [19,44]. The D band absorption intensity is observed to be less than that of the G band with the intensity ratio of ID/IG = 0.74, indicating the purity of M−CQDs having decreased oxygen vacancies on the surface of M−CQDs [24,45,46].

Fig. 6(v-vi) exhibit the Raman spectra of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (a − e), ascertaining the hexagonal wurtzite structure with the space group of C4 6v (P63mc). The pristine seed layer ZnO film (a) exhibits the characteristic vibrational modes [A1(LO) at 558, E1(TO) at 420, A1(TO) at 362, and 433 cm−1 related to E2 (high)] along with 308 cm−1 which is associated with the optical phonon overtone A1 symmetry [Fig. 6(v)]. The E2 (high) and A1(LO) modes are related to c − axis growth plane (002) of film (a) [43,47]. The unassigned peaks might be due to the residue of the precursor solution and substrate related peaks and/or annealing effect during the deposition of thin films [33,43]. The ZnO NR film (b) also exhibited the characteristics peaks [A1(LO) at 563, E1(TO) at 422, A1(TO) at 366, and 434 cm−1 associated to E2 (high) along with phonon overtone at 303 cm−1] as compared with film a. The E2 (high) peak intensity is slightly decreased which may be attributed to the crystallization of grown ZnO NRs. There are no significant changes in the Raman peak values of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e). The film (e) reveals the increased absorption intensity of E2 (high) peak, indicating that the excess amount of M−CQDs are adsorbed onto the ZnO NRs surface and/or its grain boundaries [33]. A1(LO) peak shifted to lower frequency side, attributed to the oxygen (O2) deficiency, impurity or defects in the nanocrystals and the tensile strain effect in ZnO NRs when compared to the bulk [48–50]. The Fig. 6(vi) displays that the peak intensity of D-band at 1347 cm−1 (corresponding to the defects states) of film (b) is decreased as compared with film a due to reduced defects and enhanced the crystallization of ZnO NRs. The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (c − e) exhibit the D- band peak at 1350 cm−1 and G- band at 1570 cm−1. Film e shows the increased D band peak intensity when compared to the remaining films (b, c and d) [Fig. 6(vi)], attributed to excess amount of M−CQDs with more defects and different functional groups adsorbed onto the ZnO NRs surface [50].

XPS spectra exhibit the chemical state of elements, oxidation, purity of the material and relative compositions presents in the M−CQDs powder and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films. Fig. 7 (i) represents the survey spectrum (Binding energy (BE) in the range of 0–1100 eV) of M−CQDs, exhibiting the two peaks at 284.5 eV and 530.5 eV related to the C 1s and O 1s, respectively. The Fig. 7(ii) shows the narrow spectrum of C 1s which is deconvoluted by using the gaussian fitting to reveal the carbon bonds are four types: at 282.6 eV (related to the C = C bond of sp2 graphitic structure), 284.7 associated to C–C bond, 286.2 eV due to C = O/C-O bond, and 288.3 eV (C-O). Fig. 7(iii) reveals the narrow spectrum of O 1s (deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.2 eV and 531.8 eV representing to C-O and C = O bond, respectively), ascertaining that the existence of numerous O related functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of M−CQDs in support of the FT-IR results [45,46]. The XPS survey spectra (the range of BE is 0–1200 eV) of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films are shown in the Fig. 8 (i). The BE peaks of the elements related to the ZnO and M−CQDs such as Zn, C and O are presented [47]. The core level spectrum of Zn 2p of pristine ZnO film (a) [Fig. 8(ii)] exhibits the two BE peaks at 1020.2 (Zn 2p3/2) and 1043.2 eV (Zn 2p1/2) with an energy difference of 23 eV, representing the chemical valence of Zn in the + 2-oxidization state (Zn2+ ions in Zn-O bonds). The O 1s core level spectrum exhibits the BE peak at 528 eV [deconvoluted into two peaks 528.9 (Zn-O) and 530.2 eV (C-O)] as shown in Fig. 8(iii). The Fig. 8(iv) gives the BE peak at 284.5 eV related to the adventitious carbon atoms (C 1s core level spectrum) formed while the deposition of films and/or handling of the samples under the normal ambient conditions [33,43]. The examined C 1s BE peak value (284 eV) of the film (a) is used as the charge reference for the charge correction (energy-scale calibration). Zn 2p, C 1s, and O 1s core level spectra of the corresponding peaks of ZnO NRs film (b) and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c and e) are calibrated with reference to the examined BE peak of the C 1s core level spectrum of film (a). The Fig. 8(iv) shows the C 1s (284 eV) spectrum of the film (b) and heterostructure films (c and e). The peak intensities of films (c and e) are observed to be increased as compared with film (b), ascribing to the adsorption of M−CQDs onto the ZnO NRs surface resulting in the increased surface area. The narrow spectrum of Zn 2p in film (b) exhibits the two characteristic BE peaks centred at 1020.3 (Zn 2p3/2) and 1043.4 eV (Zn 2p1/2) with the energy difference of 23 eV. The O 1s core level spectrum exhibits the 528 eV peak deconvoluted into two peaks 529 (OI) (Zn-O) and 530.7 eV (OII) (adsorbed oxygen and –OH (hydroxyl) on the ZnO NRs surface, which is slightly shifted to higher BE energy side as compared with film a and may be due to the increased surface to volume ratio of ZnO NRs [33,43]. The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (c and e) showed the Zn 2p peak values are shifted to the higher binding energy side and decreased peak intensities as compared with film b, ascribing the adsorption of M−CQDs onto the surface of ZnO NRs via chemical interactions (chemisorption) [28]. The O 1s binding energy peaks are found to be slightly shifted to higher energy side which may be related to the oxygen vacancy in the ZnO lattice engaged by the carboxyl group of M−CQDs to form Zn–COO bonds [28]. In M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films, the BEs of Zn 2p and O 1s peaks have been significantly shifted to the higher energy side, ascertaining the influential surface/interface states, quantum confinement effect, and the existence of M−CQDs on ZnO nanorod surface [28,51].

The PL spectra of M−CQDs samples and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (a − e) are shown in the Fig. 9 . M−CQDs solution exhibits the PL emission at different excitation wavelengths (λex) (320–420 nm) as given in the Fig. 9(i). It showed the emission peaking at 416 nm for the λex of 320 nm. Upon increasing of excitation wavelengths from 320 to 420 nm, its PL emission showed the characteristic red-shift with increased intensity until the λex = 360 nm and followed by the decreasing intensity with red-shift [430 at (λex − 340 nm); 443 (360 nm); 449 (380 nm); 456 (400 nm); 484 (420 nm)], attributed to the surface defects and/or sp2 hybridization in M−CQDs. The excitation dependent PL emission from UV to visible region has been ascribed to the oxygen containing functional groups, surface defect states such as (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups), radiative recombination, and the quantum confinement effect [52–54]. Fig. 9(ii) shows the normalized PL emission spectra of M−CQDs sample, showing the distinct red-shift upon increase of excitation wavelengths. M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films exhibit the PL emission upon the excitation of 325 nm (λex) as shown in the Fig. 9(iii). The pristine seed layer ZnO film (a) showed the broad emission peaked at 391 nm related to the near band-edge emission due to the free exciton recombination and at 538 nm to defect emission in the visible range corresponding to the surface defects of zinc vacancies, zinc interstitials, oxygen vacancies and oxygen interstitials [33]. The film (b) shows the emission at 389 nm with decreased peak intensity as compared with film (a), ascribed to the high surface-to-volume ratio of ZnO NRs resulting in the slightly decreased FWHM (28 nm) as compared with (32 nm) film (a). The defect level emission (539 nm) reveals decreased peak intensity as compared with film (a), indicating the enhanced crystalline structure of ZnO NRs [55]. It is also ascertained that the near-band-edge emissions of the films (c and d) are observed at 384 nm which is slightly blue-shifted with a reduced FWHM (17 nm for film c and 15 nm for film d) as compared with film b (28 nm), ascribed to the strong electronic interaction, band structure, and the formation of effective charge-transfer interface between M−CQDs and ZnO NRs [29]. There is no significant change in the defect emission wavelength but decreased intensity as compared with film b, due to the reduced surface defect states in the heterostructure attributed to the adsorption of M−CQDs on the ZnO NRs surface [51,56]. The film (e) is showing the PL emission (382 nm) with increased intensity when compared to the remaining films ascribing the excess amount of M−CQDs onto the ZnO NRs surface and increased charge-transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs [53]. The intensity of defect emissions of films (c − e) (539 nm) decreases as compared with film (b) which may be due to decreased oxygen vacancies and impurities with the decoration of CQDs onto the ZnO NRs’ surface. Fig. 9(iv) exhibits the normalized PL spectra of heterostructure films (a − e) studied at λex = 365 nm (this wavelength is used to understand the possible UV energy transfer between the M−CQDs to ZnO NRs surface in the photodetector devices). As a result of the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films (c − e) (when the ZnO NRs immersed in M−CQDs), there is an increased visible emission intensity as compared with film (a). This is due to the adsorption (chemisorption) of M−CQDs onto the ZnO surface, which absorbs the incident UV light energy and non-radiatively transfers it to the ZnO conduction band (CB) [27]. The decreased UV light intensity in the ZnO NRs to generate the charge carriers (electron-hole pairs) upon the increasing content of M−CQDs on the surface of NRs cannot be ruled out while interpreting the increased visible emission intensity of the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure films. The above results are helpful to improve the performance of UV photodetector characteristics [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV photodetectors based on M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The UV photodetector devices were fabricated based on the ZnO (film a) for the reference device A, ZnO NRs (film b) for device B and M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films (c − e) for the devices (C − E). The Fig. 10 shows the schematic diagram of device structure and energy level diagram. The J − V (current density − voltage) measurements of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs devices (A − E) are shown in the Fig. 11 under dark and UV light illumination (365 nm) with power intensity of 1 mW/cm2. The device A exhibits the dark current density of 4.7/19.3 nA/cm2 at bias (−1/1 V). Fig. 11(ii) exhibits the higher current density of 0.35/1.18 μA/cm2 (at −1/1 V) (365; 1 mW/cm2) due to the photogenerated charge carriers. The I-t characteristics (photoresponses) of the devices (A − E) are demonstrated in Fig. 12 executed by the switching (ON/OFF) of UV light for every 30 s at 1 V, exhibiting the good stability and repeatability. The device A shows the current of 0.5 μA with growth time (τg) (the time required for photocurrent rise from 10 to 90 %) of 4.8 s and fall time (τf) (the time required for photocurrent rise from 90 to 10 %) of 4.7 s as given in the Fig. 12. The mechanism of the photodetectors in dark condition as follows: Oxygen molecules adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO under normal ambient condition, by absorbing the free electrons and forming the anions (O2 (gas) + e−→ O2), creating a significant potential gradient close to the surface (producing the depletion region. When illuminating with UV light (1 mW/cm2) (switched ON), the adsorbed oxygen molecules get desorbed by the photogenerated holes (O2 −+ h+→ O2) and photogenerated electrons subsequently give rise to the photocurrent and improve the conductivity [27,29]. The main figure of merit parameters for UV photodetectors are typically its photoresponsivity (Rλ) and detectivity (Dλ). The photoresponsivity determined by using the equation Rλ = Iph/P × A where Iph is the photocurrent, P is power density of the light and A is the active area of device. The responsivity of device A showed 0.35/1.16 mA/W at bias of −1/1 V as shown in the Fig. 13 (i). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) (η) is another important parameter, which indicates the availability of generated number of electrons per incident photon. EQE calculated by using the formula η = (Rλ × hc/λ) × 100 %, where Rλ is the photoresponsivity, λ is the wavelength of the UV light. The value of EQE is found as 0.12/0.39 % at bias of −1/1 V (Fig. 13(ii)). The specific detectivity (Dλ) is calculated by the equation Dλ = Rλ/(2qJdark)½ where q is the electron charge, and Jdark is dark current density. Detectivity (Dλ) of device A as shown in the Fig. 13(iii) is 0.88/1.47 × 1010 (Jones) at −1/1 V. The photoresponsivity (Rλ), EQE (η), detectivity (Dλ), and rise & fall times of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure devices (A − E) at different voltages are given in the Table 2 .

The J − V measurements of device B as shown in the Fig. 11(i) reveal the current density of 60.6/86.7 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V under the dark. The significant increase in the dark current as compared with device A, ascribed to the improved quality of the film and increased conductivity of ZnO NRs. When illuminated with the UV light at 1 mW/cm2, the current density is found to be 90.47/103.7 μA/cm2 at voltage −1/1 V [Fig. 11(ii)]. The light current is increased by 79 times as compared with that (1.18 μA/cm2 at 1 V) of the device A due to the huge surface-to-volume ratio, leading to deep surface traps suitable for collecting charge carriers and increasing carrier lifetime for reduction in the charge recombination as supported by the PL emission results [57]. The higher current of 8.8 μA with good stability and reproducibility is measured by the I − t measurements for the device B as revealed in the Fig. 12. The increase in the growth time (16 s) and fall time (22.7 s) than that of device A may be due to the photo persistent current (when the light is switched off, the current is slowly decreasing indicating the photogenerated charge carriers available to contribute to the dark current). The device B exhibits the Rλ of 29.9/6.83 mA/W at −1/1 V and the EQE (η) of 10.14/2.32 % at bias of −1/1 V [Fig. 13(i and ii)]. The Dλ is the decreased (0.68/0.13 × 1010 Jones) when compared to that [0.88/1.47 × 1010 Jones at −1/1 V] of the device A due to the higher dark current as shown in the Fig. 13(iii). The responsivity (Rλ) and EQE are ∼ 6 times larger than to the device A.

Fig. 11(i) shows the J − V measurements of device C, showing that the current density of 41.28/42.32 μA/cm2 at bias of −1/1 V under dark which is less than that of device B, related with the increase in the width of depletion region at M−CQDs/ZnO NRs interface under the applied electric field. When illuminated with the UV light of 1 mW/cm2, it is increased to 123.8/135.7 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V as given in the Fig. 11(ii). The increasing of light current in device C indicates the presence of increased photoinduced charge carriers which are effectively transferred from M−CQDs to ZnO CB (crossing the interfacial depletion potential barrier). This is also in consistent with the PL results where the PL emission intensity is decreased due to the reduction of charge recombination and increment conductivity of the heterostructure films, resulting in the enhanced device photocurrent performances. The time dependent current measurements (I − t) exhibit the higher current (9.9 μA) with slow growth/fall time (19.8/23.1 s) than that (16/22.7 s) of the device B as shown in the Fig. 12. M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure facilitates creating the interface for the efficient charge transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs surface. The fall time is higher due to the photo persistent current (trapped surface states) [27,29]. It shows the photoresponsivity of 82.5/81.2 mA/W at voltage (−1/1 V) and the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 28.02/27.6 (%) at bias of −1/1 V [Fig. 13(i and ii)]. Using the signal-to-noise ratio calculations, the detectivity of the film is found to be 2.26/2.20 × 1010 Jones (−1/1 V), showing an enhancement in the detectivity compared to that of the device A and B [Fig. 13(iii)]. The responsivity and EQE are found to be ∼ 12 times higher as compared with device B and 70 times higher than device A at 1 V. The detectivity is also found to be 16.9 times higher than device B and 1.5 times higher than device A at 1 V [27,29,58].

The Fig. 11(i) shows the electrical behaviour of device D, exhibiting the current density of 0.05/0.07 μA/cm2 at bias of −1/1 V under the dark condition which is much less than that of the device B (60.6/86.7 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V) and C (41.28/42.32 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V). This could be due to the increased interfacial potential formed between M−CQDs and ZnO NRs and causing reduction in the dark current [27,29]. The light current is slightly decreased to 8.9/6.3 μA/cm2 under the bias of −1/1 V (Fig. 11(ii)) as compared with device C. This might be attributed to higher interface potential due to increased M−CQDs content on the surface of ZnO NRs and/or to the decrease photogenerated charge carriers in ZnO NRs because the increased absorption of incident UV light by M−CQDs on the surface [27,29]. The photo-switching measurements (Fig. 12) of device D shows the reduced current of 0.4 μA with slightly decreased growth/fall time (12.4/13.61 s) as compared with device B and C. The photoresponsivity is determined to be 8.5/6.3 mA/W at −1/1 V (Fig. 13(i)). EQE and detectivity are found to be 2.88/2.13 % and 2.2/4.22 × 1010 Jones at −1/1 V, respectively as shown in the Fig. 13(ii)-(iii).

The device E reveals the current density of 0.50/0.45 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V under the dark condition. The Fig. 11(ii) showed the light current density of 21.24/19.57 μA/cm2 at −1/1 V (365 nm, 1 mW/cm2) for device E. The decreased light current as compared with device B and C may be ascribed to the excess amount of M−CQDs adsorbed onto the ZnO NRs surface, reduction in the charge transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO CB due to the increased interface potential barrier as supported by the PL results [29]. It is also observed that the increased M−CQDs content in the device E may also create the connecting path between ZnO NRs for charge transport, resulting in the slightly increased current densities when compared to that of the device D. It showed the reduced photoresponse current with the growth/fall time of 20.7/22 s, indicating that the less number of photo-generated charge carriers are transferred from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs due to the large interface potential. It exhibited the photoresponsivity of 18.8/19.12 mA/W at −1/1V with 6.4/6.5 %, and 4.7/5.02 × 1010 Jones at −1/ 1 V [Fig. 13(i − iii)].

Due to the obvious photovoltaic property of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure, the fabricated UV-photodetectors exhibit the photocurrent without any external applied voltage (0 V). Fig. 14 reveals the photoresponse (I − t) characteristics of M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure based devices (A − E) under the switching (ON/OFF) of 365 nm with different power of 1, 3 and 5 mW/cm2) respectively. The non-zero current produced without applying any bias (0 V) ascribed to the accumulation of charge carriers at the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterojunction [59]. The pristine seed layer-based device A reveals the current values of −0.15 (at 1 mW/cm2), −0.12 (3 mW/cm2) and -0.6 nA (5 mW/cm2) at 0 V as shown in the Fig. 14. The grown ZnO NRs based device B demonstrated stable and repeatable photoresponse with the higher current of −0.33 (at 1 mW/cm2), −1.7 (3 mW/cm2) and -2.94 nA (5 mW/cm2) (Fig. 14), attributed to the large surface area of NRs and enhanced crystalline properties of ZnO NRs [59,60]. The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterojunction in the device C exhibits the stable and repeatable photoresponse with further improved photocurrent of −0.5 (at 1 mW/cm2), −2.3 (3 mW/cm2) and -3.27 nA (5 mW/cm2). The enhanced photocurrent as compared with device B may be due to the adsorption of M−CQDs onto the ZnO NRs surface, creating the surface depletion layer and the efficient energy transfer (photogenerated charge carriers) from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs [53]. The devices D does not give any significant photoresponse current which may be ascribed to the fact that upon increasing M−CQDs content, the width of the depletion region formed at M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterojunction gets increased and resulting in the higher potential barrier for limiting the charge transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs in addition to the decreased photogenerated charge carriers in ZnO NRs due to the adsorption of excess amount of M−CQDs onto ZnO NRs surface [29]. The further increase of M−CQDs content onto the surface of ZnO NRs results in further reduced photocurrent as compared with device C, supporting the further increased width of depletion region at the heterojunction (increased potential barrier) to prevent the energy transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO [27,29]. The transfer of photogenerated charge carriers from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs and the increased interface potential barrier at the M−CQDs/ZnO heterojunction upon increasing content of M−CQDs in the devices (C − E) are schematically represented in the Fig. 14(i − ii). The self-powered UV photodetector shows the detectivity of 2.37 × 108 under light intensity of 1 mW/cm2 for the device B at 0 V. The device C exhibits the increased detectivity of 3.27 × 108 Jones as compared with device B at 0 V, attributed to the significant charge transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs at the optimal level of M−CQDs content.

These results show that the M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin films exhibit the good UV photodetector performance at 1 V (365 nm of 1 mW/cm2). The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure based UV photodetector (device C) with optimum level of M−CQDs content shows the improved photoresponsivity and EQE both are 12 times higher than device B and 70 times higher than to the device A, ascertaining the effective charge transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO in support of PL studies and resulting in the increased device performances at 1 V. This M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure also exhibits the significant photoresponse current at 0 V which can also be used as a self-powered device for the cost-effective UV photodetector applications.

The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure thin film-based UV photodetector devices exhibit the higher photoresponse at zero bias (0 V) (self-power mode) under the UV illumination of 365 nm. M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure device also shows the higher photocurrent density of 123.49 μA/cm2 at 1 V (UV 365 nm with power density of 1 mW/cm2) as compared with ZnO NRs (93.5 μA/cm2) and ZnO seed layer (1.17 μA/cm2) devices. It indeed shows the higher photoresponsivity of 81.2 mA/W and EQE of 27.6 % at 1 V, attributed to the efficient energy transfer from M−CQDs to ZnO NRs. The M−CQDs/ZnO NRs heterostructure demonstrates the improved photoresponse (-0.5 nA) at 0 V (1 mW/cm2) which is 1.5 times higher than that (0.33 nA) of ZnO NRs based device, showing its effective use in the cost effective self-powered devices.
